text: Psychologists David Meyer and Joshua Rubinstein identified what they called "switch costs" — the time lost each time a person shifts attention from one task to another. Their studies found that even brief mental blocks created by task-switching can cost as much as 40 percent of productive time. The cost is highest when tasks are complex and unfamiliar; the brain must not only reorient itself but also suppress the residual activation of the previous task. This interference, which Meyer termed "blocking," is not a character flaw but a structural feature of human cognition that no amount of practice fully eliminates.
question: What did Meyer and Rubinstein find about how much productive time task-switching can cost?

options.text: Up to 25 percent of productive time
Up to 40 percent of productive time
Up to 60 percent of productive time
Up to 15 percent of productive time
